title: Cleaning and restoring a leather sofa
subtitle: Clean surface grime with a leather cleaner, feed dried leather with conditioner, and touch up worn colour
excerpt: How to clean, condition, and restore a leather sofa that has become grubby, stiff, or faded. Covers the correct order of cleaning and conditioning, how to test products before using them on the full sofa, and touch-up colour application for worn areas.

glossaryTerms.definition: Aniline leather is dyed through and has no surface coating, it absorbs water and shows a dark wet patch when tested. Pigmented leather has a polymer colour coat on the surface, a water droplet beads. The distinction matters for product choice: aniline leather is damaged by solvent cleaners; pigmented leather tolerates them.

An emulsified wax or oil product that replaces the natural oils that evaporate from leather over time. Unconditioned leather becomes stiff and cracks along flex lines. Conditioning is done after cleaning, not before, conditioner pushed into dirty leather traps the dirt.

content.text: Most leather sofas in UK homes are

, a tougher hide with a protective polymer surface coat. The test is a water drop: if it beads, it is pigmented; if it darkens and absorbs, it is aniline. The cleaning approach below is for pigmented leather, which covers the majority of domestic sofas.
Step 1, Test products in a hidden area

Before applying any product to a visible panel, test on the back of the sofa or under a cushion. Apply a small amount, rub gently, and leave for 10 minutes. Check for colour bleed, surface dulling, or texture change. Any of these means the product is wrong for this leather, try a milder cleaner or consult the sofa manufacturer.

Apply a small amount of leather cleaner (pH-neutral, suitable for pigmented leather) to a barely damp microfibre cloth. Work in sections, rubbing in small circles, the cloth will pick up a grey or brown tint as it lifts body oils, skin cells, and dust. Use a fresh cloth section for each panel. Do not use washing-up liquid, saddle soap on pigmented leather, or baby wipes, all are too harsh for the surface coat or deposit residue that attracts dirt.
Allow the leather to dry for 30 minutes after cleaning. Apply

with a clean cloth in a thin even coat, do not flood the surface. Work the conditioner into seams and any fold creases, which are the first areas to crack. Leave for 20 minutes, then buff off the excess with a dry cloth. Overapplication leaves a tacky surface that attracts dust.
Step 4, Touch up worn colour areas

For armrests or headrests where the pigment coat has worn through, apply a leather colourant (water-based, available in matched shades from leather repair kits) with a small sponge in multiple thin coats, one heavy coat cracks. Allow each coat to dry (10 to 15 minutes) before the next. Two or three thin coats rebuild the colour without a thick painted look. Finish with a leather protection cream to seal the new colour.
Aniline leather: use only products labelled for aniline or unprotected leather. The conditioner for aniline leather is a pure oil, not the polymer type used for pigmented hides.

Ink or biro marks: apply a small amount of surgical spirit (70% isopropanol) with a cotton bud directly on the mark only, not the surrounding leather. Ink in pigmented leather sits on the surface coat; it often lifts without touching the colour beneath.
